## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hey, this is Deepash and this video is all about retargeting. And when it comes to retargeting with Facebook ads, and especially in the framework that I use, I'm talking about people that have visited the site. So you can remarket to people that have viewed a video, engaged with your page, et cetera, et cetera. And I consider those a warm audience. And I put that in my warm audience targeting. And they're people that have somehow engaged but not clicked, retargeting for me is people that have clicked into the site. So they might have viewed a landing page, added to cart, abandoned their checkout, et cetera. This is what this video is all about. So there's two ways I tackle this. When it's a fairly new store and I don't have enough data or there's just not enough data in terms of number of customers that have come through, I set up a retargeting campaign that's quite broad. So I might just generally go, people that haven't purchased unless zero to seven to 30 days that have visited my site and then push them into retargeting pool because the bigger the audience, the better. Now, as the audience size starts to grow, as you start to spend more money on your ads, then you can be a bit more targeted and maybe split out people that have visited in the first three days or seven days and seven to 10 days, 10 to 14 days and create different segments of your audience to include in retargeting and split out the message in particular. But when your audiences are small and when you don't really have different messages to share, then it's not really beneficial to split that audience out. So I'll give you an example on this particular account here which is still failing you. So this is the campaign I've set up. So it's a product catalog campaign to retarget people that have viewed a product or added to cart but not purchase. So the first campaign I set up was actually retargeting people that have visited the site and not purchased two days ago or up to 30 days. Now, the reason I do this is it comes back to Facebook data attribution. When you're running ads in your cold audience, if you run an ad and someone clicks, then they disappear and go onto Facebook, then they see your retargeting ad, then they click or they just go back and buy anyway because of the view you're at. Then the ad that gets attributed to sale is your retargeting ad. But in reality, that retargeting ad might not have had a big play in converting someone into a purchase. And so what I do especially for smaller stores and also bigger stores that I generally know that people are gonna buy in their first day, if not the first visit, that I don't hit them up with retargeting ads. And that just means that if people do click a cold ad, a prospecting ad and they purchase in the same day through the same ad or otherwise, then the attribution of the sale goes to my cold ad.

## [03:04 - 06:04]

If this doesn't make sense, I would suggest kind of going back through the full final framework, understanding what cold, warm, hot audiences are. Now, when you're running prospecting ads, the, so the Facebook works on a last click attribution when it comes to ads. So what I want you to kind of take away is, if someone sees a prospecting ad, they click on it, they visit your site, disappear, then they see a retargeting ad, click on that and then buy it in the same day, then your retargeting ad is gonna get the sale. And it looks like retargeting is working. And you know, for all intents and purposes, that's great. But it doesn't help the performance of your cold targeting your ad set because it's not getting the hit back in terms of a conversion. So that's why I set up my audience this way. And essentially, I'm just kind of excluding anyone that's visited in the last day. So this is a kind of set up for this particular style of retargeting. So it's a dynamic product catalog, campaign objective. I've set up my ad set to retarget people that have viewed my site. I've set up a custom target here. So I've selected a catalog, which is items over $20 because I don't want cheaper items bringing people back in and dropping my average order value, which does happen. And then I'm including people that have viewed a product or added to basket in the last 30 days, but have not purchased in the last 14 days and they've not viewed or added to basket in the last one day, one day even. So that just means I'm only retargeting people the second day after they haven't purchased after the clicks. And also for retargeting, I go for all placements because especially when you don't have enough data, there's not enough to really kind of make conclusions or sense of. So there's no point in really doing anything much with placements or segmentation. And also generally for retargeting, I find that the auto placements works better as well. Now in this example here, as we have been doing some testing, I've then introduced a 10% offer. And I want to target that to people five days after the first visit and no longer than 10 days after the first visit of the view content event or the Antikarp. Now, why am I doing five days and not two days and three days or whatever it is? There's no reason. It's just my first start point to test. And I've already got data coming through. And if I look at click through rate, for example, which is somewhere here. So actually the click through rate for retargeting and especially with 10% off is really, really good. So people are definitely coming back. Now this particular store, there's some challenges on the product page. And that's why I know we are struggling to convert on a goal of $10 or below cost-provided cart. But I know that that 10% offer is working. ## [06:04 - 09:05]

A lot of you that are running ads for your own store or for a client store will feel obliged to offer a discount for people that have not purchased. But you know, test it. I don't think in many cases you have to. Some people are just gonna buy anyway. There's different types of buyers out there. There's those kind of rabid buyers that love the product they just want to buy. There's those buyers that just need a nudge and a hint. You know, it could be social proof. It could be testimonials depending on the type of product. Could be even an unboxing video. They just need convincing. And then there's those buyers that are really, really hard to convince. And they just need something bigger to convince them. And then that's where the offers come through. So when I'm setting up my retargeting, I'm thinking about how can I get them back without devaluing or dropping my average order value? And then I'm kind of giving a few more days and then potentially introducing offers as well. I don't really like to go deep into offers because especially if you're trying to build a brand, if you continually discount, it's really hard to bring people in at a decent price and keep your profits high as well. So this is a really simple example of an early on E-com setup. Now, in terms of understanding when people buy, you can actually do that through Facebook analytics. So if you haven't used analytics yet, head over to your menu and have a look at analytics. There's a ton of stuff here and there is a separate training on this. But in particular, I'm looking at funnels. So I've set this particular funnel up and I've started with page views because I want anyone that's viewed anything on my site. And then I've created my mini funnel. So this is my view content, add to cart, initiate, checkout, and purchase. And what I can do is look down that funnel. I can see conversion rates, there's some cool stuff that I can look at. But what I'm actually looking for is what is my median completion time? So the median is kind of the middle value of how long it takes people to convert through this funnel, which is viewing any page on my site and actually purchasing, it's about half a day. So I know, for example, most people that see an ad for the first time or see an ad and visit the site will purchase, if they're going to purchase, within 12 hours. And so in this particular form, this strategy would work well for me because if I'm converting people through my cold ads and they're buying within 12 hours, then all of that juicy data gets sent back to my cold audiences. Then if after day two, they don't purchase, and you can see there's many people that don't purchase as a percentage of overall page views, as 97%, then for them, I have my retargeting setup and then I start my retargeting sequence. In terms of retargeting content, I would really be playing around a lot with testimonials. So even putting star ratings and saying, this is kind of five out of five, putting some quotes and unboxing, et cetera, that makes a huge difference in terms of the types of ads that people react to.

## [09:05 - 12:08]

But the type of approach is really to make sure that you're testing, you're testing different ideas out. I don't think there's a one-size-fits-all when it comes to retargeting. I've seen some people absolutely recommend that, you do this between zero and three days, then three to seven, you do this, seven to 14, you do that. It has to be right for your products and your brand, and that's about testing. Then once you've got more data, and this is just to kind of show you an account that's slightly more matured, then you can start to split things out and be a bit more specific. So for example, this one, we have a one to 14 day retargeting in place and then a 14 to 28 day. And we've got different messages, but also different budgets that we manage. So you can see, for example, here, we're spending not a lot on this. It's about 25 pounds, I think that relates to, it's about $30, $40. And that's generating an 84% return on hand spend. So that's 8,400%. And that's a really small spend because we're testing and we're taking things over at the moment. And that's between one and 14 days. And there's an even smaller spend after that because we know the return generally is smaller, but we're just doing small tests. And we're changing up the messaging as well. So from one to 14 days, we're really trying to work on fear of missing out, how great the product is, et cetera. If after 14 days that haven't converted, we're trying the different tax. So we're talking about reintroducing some of the virtues that we brought in our cold audience targeting. So for this particular store, it was how cool the brand is, how great the products are, et cetera. So we're flexing different messages through our retargeting funnel. Then if after 28 days here, from their last click they haven't purchased, then they fall back into our cold audience targeting, our prospecting. So our prospecting ads will exclude anyone that's visited in the last 28 days. But if after 28 days of their first click they haven't purchased, then they end up seeing our cold ads again. Now in some cases, you could end up serving them nothing. And you can do that by in your cold audience targeting, just exclude people that clicked in the last 35 days or 40 days, which means if they go through your retargeting sequence and still haven't bought after 28 days, they might get five or 10 days of seeing no ads and then you start to reintroduce it again. And that's often a softer way of trying to bring people back through the funnel because if they haven't converted, they just might not be right for you as well. So there are a few tactics there for retargeting. And essentially the key thing to take away from this is think about getting the audience size, right? Start testing with bigger audiences and then start to segment it out. So for example, if I take these items, these ad sets here, I can then start breaking it down and actually seeing, can I see anything specific about who's actually converting?

## [12:08 - 15:10]

And if my audiences are big enough, then I could potentially split them out. If you look here, actually Facebook is pushing more spend to female 25, 34 than it is to female 18, 24. It could be that there's more people matching that in the audience, but I wanna control that. Cause if I'm getting such a high return on ad spend, I wanna pull out these 18 to 24s, serve them different retargeting audience and manage the budget more effectively by pushing more budget there and actually reducing the budget for this particular audience. ## [15:10 - 16:14]

that you can get enough conversions a week to get stability. If you're optimizing for purchase, you're not getting 50, then I would consider dropping that down. One of the key things about retargeting is the audience is super, super hot in most cases. You don't have to put the purchase event because you literally want pretty much everyone to come back. So in this kind of instance, a few things that you can actually play around with is actually optimizing for the top of the funnel event. So view content or landing page views. If the audience is small, I'll often test impressions or daily unique reach. And just to make sure that the ad is getting delivered as well. You can also, and I think this is probably one of the only scenarios I suggest this, is use a traffic campaign objective. Because the traffic campaign, the goal is just to get as many clicks as possible. And if you combine that with paying per click and not by impression, you can actually improve your cost per acquisition, your price even further. So there are a few different tactics that play around with retargeting as well.
